concepts.name: Insulin
concepts.blurb: Moves glucose from blood into cells. Sensitivity = how cleanly cells respond to that signal.
concepts.good_when: Stable energy through the day, no post-meal crashes, less hunger and fewer cravings, easier to stay lean.
concepts.bad_when: Resistance: pancreas pumps out a lot, blood sugar stays elevated. Drift toward type 2 diabetes, weight gain, cardiovascular disease, even Alzheimer's (sometimes called 'type 3 diabetes').
details: Muscle is your biggest glucose sink — more muscle means more storage capacity for carbs.
Heavy lifting depletes muscle glycogen, creating 'demand' — for 24–48 hours after, your muscles pull glucose out of the bloodstream very efficiently.
Muscle contraction itself triggers glucose uptake without insulin (GLUT4 pathway). Diabetics often see immediate blood sugar drops after lifting.

concepts.name: Ghrelin
concepts.blurb: The hunger hormone. Made in the stomach lining; rises before meals, drops after eating.
details: Ghrelin is *trained* by your eating patterns. Eat at 8am consistently → your stomach starts releasing ghrelin around 7:30 → you feel hungry on schedule.
Skip breakfast for weeks → your stomach stops the morning release → you don't feel hungry. Doesn't mean you don't need food. Means your hunger signaling has been retrained off-pattern.
'Naturally lean' guys often have quiet ghrelin signaling because the body adapted to chronic underfeeding. Installing a regular protein breakfast for 2–3 weeks reactivates morning hunger.

concepts.name: Leptin
concepts.blurb: The fullness hormone. Tells you 'I'm full' — opposite of ghrelin.
details: Skipping meals confuses leptin signaling over time.
Regular protein-anchored meals keep both ghrelin and leptin firing on a clean rhythm — real hunger when you should be hungry, real fullness when you should be full.

concepts.name: Cortisol
concepts.blurb: The stress hormone — but also the wake-up hormone. Naturally peaks ~30 min after waking.
concepts.good_when: Healthy morning peak that gets you out of bed.
concepts.bad_when: Chronically elevated. Suppresses testosterone, tanks thyroid function, disrupts sleep, hurts body composition (especially abdominal fat retention), worsens mood, suppresses immunity.
raises_chronic: Skipping meals (body interprets fasting as continuing stress)
lowers_chronic: Resistance training (acutely stressful, but lowers chronic baseline over weeks)
Sauna and cold exposure (acute spike, lower baseline over time)

concepts.name: Testosterone
concepts.blurb: The male sex hormone. Drives muscle growth, libido, drive, mood, recovery.
details: Realistic numbers: a sedentary guy with poor sleep, high body fat, and high stress who fixes those things might see total T go from ~350 ng/dL to ~550 ng/dL. Real, life-changing improvement.
What lifting WON'T do: double your testosterone, take you to the levels of a 22-year-old, or fix genuinely low T (under ~300 with symptoms — that's a doctor situation).
raised_by: Lower body fat (fat tissue contains aromatase, which converts T to estrogen)
Better sleep (most T production happens during deep sleep)
Resistance training, especially heavy compound lifts
Adequate calorie intake (severe deficits crash T)

concepts.name: Growth Hormone
concepts.blurb: Released during deep sleep and acutely after heavy lifting. Supports tissue repair, fat metabolism, recovery.
details: The biggest GH driver in your day is the first deep sleep cycle of the night.
Lifting hard then sleeping poorly squanders most of the recovery benefit.

concepts.name: Muscle Protein Synthesis
concepts.blurb: Muscle isn't continuously built — it's built in waves triggered by meals.
details: A protein-rich meal launches an MPS wave that lasts ~3–5 hours, then drops back to baseline.
You can't stack waves. Eating 80g of protein in one sitting doesn't double the response of 40g — there's a per-meal ceiling.
Optimal pattern: 3–4 meals/day, each with 30–40g of complete protein, spaced 4–5 hours apart.
Skip breakfast → skip an entire MPS wave that day. Over a year, that's significant muscle you didn't build.
amplified_by: Training that day (training amplifies MPS for ~24 hours)

concepts.name: Leucine
concepts.blurb: An essential amino acid — the one that flips the MPS switch.
details: Hit ~2.5–3g of leucine per meal to trigger a maximal MPS response.
Leucine is ~8–10% of high-quality protein. So a meal with 25–30g of complete protein has the leucine you need. Don't track leucine directly — just hit 30–40g of varied protein per meal.
Animal proteins, dairy, eggs, soy: complete amino profile, hit threshold easily.
Most plant proteins: lower leucine — eat ~40g/meal to match.

concepts.name: Glycogen
concepts.blurb: How the body stores carbs. Fuel for the bar.
details: When you eat carbs, your body breaks them into glucose; some goes to immediate use, the rest is stored as glycogen — ~400g in muscles and ~100g in the liver.
Heavy lifting primarily burns muscle glycogen. When it's depleted, you feel weak, flat, sluggish — and your lifts will be terrible.
Carbs aren't optional for a lifter. They're the fuel system the work runs on.

concepts.name: The Tension Threshold
concepts.blurb: Muscle adaptation requires high mechanical tension on a fiber, sustained long enough to cause micro-damage and metabolic stress. The threshold is roughly 60–85% of one-rep max.
details: Below the threshold, you can move weight all day and the signal is weak. Above it, the signal is loud and clear.
This is why a sweaty circuit class with 15-lb dumbbells — regardless of how exhausting — does not build meaningful strength or muscle. It's below the tension threshold.

concepts.name: Default Mode Network
concepts.blurb: Your brain's 'rest mode'. Active when your mind wanders — handles emotional processing, future planning, self-reflection, creative insight, identity formation. The 'shower thought' effect.
details: Two basic modes: task-positive (focused on something) and DMN (mind-wandering).
Modern life starves the DMN — phones, podcasts, music, constant input keep the task-positive network running.
starvation_signs: Struggling to know what you actually want

concepts.name: Vagus Nerve
concepts.blurb: The main highway from brain to organs. Switching it on shifts you from sympathetic (fight/flight) toward parasympathetic (rest/digest).
details: You can directly engage it through breath rhythm — the only autonomic process under conscious control.
Long walks slightly slow and deepen breathing, nudging the same shift.

practices.name: Heavy Lifting
practices.blurb: The strongest legal signal you can send the body to build muscle and improve metabolic health.
how: Compound barbell lifts (squat, deadlift, bench, OHP, row).
Load the most muscle at once.
Allow heavy loading safely — your legs and back can handle real weight.
Are progressive — you can add 5 lbs to a barbell.
Train the nervous system, not just muscle.
Time-efficient — one movement, one stimulus, multiple muscle groups.
practices.when: Any time of day, but consistency matters more than timing.

practices.name: Walking
how: Insulin sensitivity boost — a walk after meals dramatically improves how your body handles glucose for the next several hours.
Active recovery — increases blood flow to recovering tissue, clearing waste and delivering nutrients.
Joint and tendon health — connective tissue has poor blood supply and depends on movement for nutrients.
Cardiovascular maintenance — lifting builds strength, not endurance. Walking handles the cardio side gently.
Engages the Default Mode Network. Phone-free is the key.
Bilateral movement reduces amygdala activity (same mechanism EMDR therapy uses).
Vagus nerve activation — long walks slow and deepen breathing.
Sunlight exposure — even on cloudy days, outdoor light is 10–100× brighter than indoor light, calibrating circadian rhythm.
practices.when: After meals for the glucose effect. Phone-free for the mental effect.

practices.name: Sleep
practices.blurb: The master lever. Skimping on sleep undoes lifting, dieting, and emotional regulation faster than anything else.
details: Most testosterone is produced in deep sleep.
Most growth hormone is released in the first deep sleep cycle.
Glymphatic system clears metabolic waste from the brain.
Muscle tissue repairs and rebuilds.
Memory consolidates.
Emotional processing happens during REM.
architecture: Sleep cycles through 4 stages, ~90 minutes each.
N2 — light sleep, heart rate slows, body cools.
N3 — deep sleep, physical repair, GH release, immune function.
REM — dreams, emotional processing, memory consolidation.
Earlier cycles are heavier on deep sleep. Later cycles are heavier on REM. Going to bed late steals deep sleep; waking early steals REM.
helpers: Sauna 90 min before bed (drops core temp afterward)
Consistent wake time (more important than consistent bedtime)
blockers: Alcohol — destroys REM sleep even at small doses
THC — suppresses REM, even though it makes you fall asleep faster

practices.name: Cold Exposure
practices.dose: 30–60 seconds at the end of a normal shower
practices.blurb: Acute discomfort with disproportionate downstream benefits.
how: Triggers a norepinephrine spike → focus and alertness for hours.
Mild antidepressant effect with regular use.
Discomfort tolerance training — choosing something hard you don't want to do every morning carries over to the rest of life.
warnings: Skip immediately post-lifting. Cold blunts the muscle growth signal for ~4 hours.

practices.name: Sauna
practices.blurb: Strong evidence for cardiovascular and cognitive benefits.
how: Cardiovascular mortality reduction (large Finnish studies).
Reduced dementia and Alzheimer's risk.
Improved heat tolerance and cardiovascular fitness.
Mild GH bump.
Recovery acceleration.
practices.when: After lifting or in the evening. Hydrate before and after — water plus a pinch of salt is ideal.

practices.name: Box Breathing
practices.blurb: The fastest way to switch nervous-system state. Used by Navy SEALs for pre-mission stress regulation.
how: Inhale 4 seconds, hold 4, exhale 4, hold 4. Repeat.
The rhythm directly engages the vagus nerve, shifting you from sympathetic (stress) to parasympathetic (calm).
Heart rate slows, mind quiets, anxious buzz drops.
when: Before bed if mind is racing

practices.name: Nasal Breathing
how: Reduces stress hormones.
Dries airways less.
Improves sleep architecture.
Mouth-breathing increases cortisol and worsens sleep.

practices.name: Protein-Anchored Meals
practices.dose: 3–4 meals/day, 30–40g of complete protein each, 4–5 hours apart
practices.blurb: The eating pattern that drives the muscle-building machinery.
how: Each meal launches a fresh MPS wave.
30–40g of complete protein clears the leucine threshold.
Skip a meal → skip a wave that day.
Spacing matters: <3 hours between meals = waves overlap and the second one is wasted.
Animal proteins, dairy, eggs, soy hit the threshold easily. Most plant proteins need ~40g.

practices.name: Sunlight Exposure
practices.blurb: Calibrates circadian rhythm and supports vitamin D, which underpins T.
how: Even on cloudy days, outdoor light is 10–100× brighter than indoor light.
Morning sunlight anchors the circadian clock — better evening sleep onset.
Vitamin D synthesis from skin exposure (most men are low).

practices.name: Progressive Overload
practices.dose: +5 lb per session on compounds; +2.5 lb on OHP
practices.blurb: The adaptation engine. Slightly more weight or reps, week over week.
how: Without it, adaptation stalls. With it, adaptation continues for years.
This is why the program is structured around adding 5 lbs per session.
Failed reps → repeat the same weight. 3 consecutive failures → deload by 10%.

principles.name: Signals, not commands
principles.blurb: The body is a self-regulating system that responds to signals — not a machine you push harder to get more out of. Lifting, food, sleep, sun, movement, stress — all signals. Get them right and the body adapts in your favor.
details: Almost everything in this guide is about understanding what signal you're sending and what adaptation it triggers.
When something isn't working, the question isn't 'how do I push harder' — it's 'what signal am I sending, and what adaptation is the body actually making?'

principles.name: Multiplicative compounding
principles.blurb: The compounded effect of doing this stuff isn't additive — it's multiplicative. Lifting alone is good. Lifting + protein + sleep + walking + sauna + sun + breath work is dramatically better than any single piece.
details: Most guys get fragmentary results because they pick one or two and ignore the rest.
The whole stack working together is what produces real change — not just in the body, but in mood, presence, confidence, and how you show up to the world.

The point of understanding the mechanisms isn't to be clever about it. It's so when life knocks you off the program — bad sleep week, travel, stress at work — you can think clearly about what's happening and what to prioritize next, instead of feeling defeated.

edges.note: Depletes glycogen → muscles pull glucose efficiently for 24–48h.
Acutely stressful, but lowers chronic baseline over weeks.
MPS amplified for ~24 hours after training.
Most T production happens in deep sleep.
Largest release in first deep sleep cycle.
Sleep quality the night before increases the response.
~4 hour window. Skip immediately after lifting.
90 min before bed drops core temp afterward, signals sleep.
Each meal launches a fresh wave.
Regular protein meals retrain morning hunger.
